summary: Global equity markets are displaying a cautiously optimistic tone, with Wall Street edging higher led by technology sector gains even as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East inject significant uncertainty into investor sentiment. The primary macro drivers include escalating conflict between Israel and Lebanon, with Netanyahu ordering strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs, while U.S.-Iran nuclear talks remain fragile after Iran signaled it may halt message exchanges with Washington and threatened to block the Strait of Hormuz. On the corporate front, FedEx Freight received a fresh buy rating following its spinoff, JetBlue flagged rising fuel costs tied to the Iran conflict, the UAW strike is threatening General Motors truck production, and Barry Diller's People is reportedly preparing an $18 billion bid for MGM Resorts. The chief risk factors commanding investor attention are a potential Hormuz blockade that could severely disrupt global oil supply chains, soaring factory input costs from supply shocks, and a comparison to the 2000 dotcom bubble top that has rattled some market participants, while gold's nearly 2% decline suggests inflation hedging dynamics are shifting. In the near term, markets will be closely watching the trajectory of U.S.-Iran diplomacy, the May jobs report — which prediction markets suggest may beat expectations — and whether U.S. manufacturing's four-year high activity level can be sustained against mounting supply constraints and geopolitical headwinds.
